content.text: The org design question for GenAI delivery is not "how many AI engineers do we hire?" — it is "where do we put them, who do they report to, and how do they coordinate across business units?" The wrong answer creates either a bottleneck (all centralized) or chaos (fully distributed with no shared standards). The right answer depends on the organization's size, maturity, regulatory exposure, and how fast individual BUs need to move.

attrs.alt: Three GenAI org design patterns: Centralized (single team serves all BUs — strong governance, bottleneck risk), Hub-and-Spoke (CoE plus embedded BU guilds — balances governance and autonomy), Federated (BUs own their AI, policy office sets rules only — max autonomy, inconsistent governance)

content.text: The Three Design Patterns
Most enterprise GenAI org designs fall into one of three archetypes. These are not rigid categories — real organizations blend them — but the three patterns define the decision space:
Pattern
Where AI expertise lives
Who owns platform?
Best fit
Centralized
Single GenAI team; all BUs are consumers
Central team exclusively
Regulated industries (FSI, healthcare); org with < 4 BUs; Year 1 of AI adoption
Hub-and-Spoke
CoE hub + embedded guild leads in each BU
CoE owns shared platform; BUs own feature layer
Most mid-to-large enterprises; organizations balancing governance and speed
Federated
Each BU has its own AI team; minimal central function
Each BU independently
Tech-native conglomerates; orgs with large, autonomous P&L-owning BUs; mature GenAI capability
The
hub-and-spoke model

is the right target state for most enterprises for one reason: it is the only pattern that simultaneously solves the two hardest problems in enterprise GenAI delivery — consistent governance (hub) and speed to production in each BU's specific context (spoke). The other two patterns solve one at the cost of the other.
Target-State: Hub-and-Spoke in Depth
Hub: GenAI Center of Excellence
The hub is a small, senior team — typically 5–10 people in a 5,000-person organization — that owns the shared capabilities every BU depends on:
Hub function
What it owns
What it does not own
Platform engineering
LLM gateway, model registry, guardrail gateway, LLMOps pipeline, vector store service, observability stack
BU-specific application code, feature pipelines, or BU data infrastructure
Technical standards
Prompt design guide, RAG architecture patterns, evaluation runbook, agent safety checklist
BU implementation choices within approved patterns
Governance
Acceptable-use policy, risk register, Tier 2 gate reviews, exec reporting
Product roadmap decisions, BU hiring decisions
Enablement
Training curriculum, certifications, internal community, office hours
Team-specific onboarding or mentoring beyond guild lead level
Spoke: Embedded AI Guilds
Each BU with meaningful GenAI ambition has an embedded AI guild — 2 to 5 engineers who are permanent members of the BU's engineering organization but participate in CoE governance:
Guild leads attend the bi-weekly CoE guild sync
Guild members complete CoE certifications and are the BU's first-line advisors for GenAI questions
Guild leads pre-screen BU use cases against Tier 2 triggers before submitting intake forms
Guild members build on the CoE platform (LLM gateway, eval harness) rather than re-implementing shared infrastructure
Guild leads represent the BU's needs in the CoE platform roadmap process

The guild lead's reporting line is the critical design decision: they report to the BU's engineering leader for day-to-day work and career management, but they are accountable to the CoE lead for compliance with governance. This
dotted-line accountability
creates healthy tension — the guild lead cannot silently ignore a Tier 2 gate requirement, but the CoE cannot unilaterally remove them from BU projects.
Staffing the Hub-and-Spoke Model
Hub: Role Definitions and Sizing

# Indicative hub headcount model for hub-and-spoke org design.
# These are rough guidelines — adjust based on BU count, maturity, and risk profile.

def hub_headcount(num_bus: int, monthly_api_calls_millions: float, tier2_cases_per_quarter: int) -> dict:
    """
    Estimate minimum viable hub headcount.
    Does not include guild members (BU-funded) or legal/compliance (separate dept).
    """
    # Platform engineering scales with usage, not BU count
    platform_eng = max(2, round(monthly_api_calls_millions / 10))

    # Advisory scales with BU count and gate volume
    advisory = max(1, round((num_bus * 0.5) + (tier2_cases_per_quarter / 10)))

    return {
        "coe_lead": 1,
        "principal_ai_architect": max(1, round(num_bus / 5)),
        "ai_ethics_lead": 1,
        "mlops_lmmops_engineers": platform_eng,
        "coe_program_manager": 1,
        "advisory_architects": advisory,
        "total": 1 + max(1, round(num_bus / 5)) + 1 + platform_eng + 1 + advisory,
    }

# Examples
print(hub_headcount(4, 5.0, 8))
# {'coe_lead': 1, 'principal_ai_architect': 1, 'ai_ethics_lead': 1,
#  'mlops_lmmops_engineers': 2, 'coe_program_manager': 1, 'advisory_architects': 2, 'total': 8}

print(hub_headcount(12, 50.0, 30))
# {'coe_lead': 1, 'principal_ai_architect': 2, 'ai_ethics_lead': 1,
#  'mlops_lmmops_engineers': 5, 'coe_program_manager': 1, 'advisory_architects': 4, 'total': 14}
Spoke: Guild Sizing by BU Maturity
BU maturity
Characteristics
Guild size
Guild lead profile
Exploring
First GenAI project; no production deployments
1 guild lead (part-time)
Senior engineer with ML background; completing CoE certifications
Scaling
1–5 use cases in production; regular intake submissions
2–4 guild members + 1 guild lead
Staff engineer; CoE-certified; attends all governance forums
Optimizing
> 5 production use cases; complex agentic systems
4–8 guild members + 1 guild lead; may have a dedicated AI PM
Principal engineer; co-designs CoE standards; chairs guild sync quarterly

BU maturity is not static. A BU that was Exploring in Q1 may be Scaling by Q3 if a use case lands well. The CoE should review BU maturity classifications quarterly and adjust guild resourcing recommendations — this is an input to the CoE's quarterly exec report.
Transition: From Current State to Target State

Most organizations start in an accidental centralized model — a handful of AI enthusiasts in one team who everyone treats as the AI team by default. Getting to hub-and-spoke requires a deliberate transition with three phases:
Phase 1: Establish the Hub (Months 1–6)
Ratify the CoE charter with the Executive Steering Committee
Hire or appoint the CoE Lead, Principal AI Architect, and MLOps Lead
Stand up the LLM gateway and model registry; route all production model calls through it
Publish the first version of the acceptable-use policy and Tier 2 gate criteria
Run the first GenAI Foundations training cohort
Success criteria for Phase 1:
100% of new production LLM calls routed through the gateway
; at least one use case approved through the formal Tier 2 process; first training cohort delivered with > 20 completions.
Phase 2: Establish the Spokes (Months 4–12, overlapping Phase 1)
Identify one guild lead candidate in each of the top 3–4 BUs by GenAI ambition
Guild leads complete CoE certifications and attend the bi-weekly guild sync
BUs with active use cases submit their first formal intake requests through the CoE process
CoE platform adds the first BU-requested feature based on guild input (demonstrates that the platform is responsive)
Success criteria for Phase 2:
all BUs with production GenAI have a named, certified guild lead
; intake process is the normal path (not the exception) for new use cases; at least one cross-BU pattern reuse documented.
Phase 3: Optimize and Automate (Month 12+)
Introduce showback billing so BUs see their platform consumption costs
Build BU self-service for Tier 3 advisory (documentation, automated checks) so CoE capacity focuses on Tier 2
Promote top-performing guild leads to contribute to CoE standards authorship
Evaluate which BUs are ready to manage their own feature-layer deployments with CoE platform as substrate
Decision Framework: Choosing the Right Pattern
If you are not sure which pattern fits, use this decision tree:
Question
Yes → consider
No → consider
Are you in a regulated industry (FSI, healthcare, insurance) with strict AI governance requirements?
Centralized or Hub-and-Spoke (lean toward hub for scale)
Hub-and-Spoke or Federated
Do you have more than 4 distinct business units with active GenAI programs?
Hub-and-Spoke (centralized won't scale)
Centralized may work in Year 1
Do individual BUs have strong P&L ownership and the ability to hire independently?
Hub-and-Spoke or Federated (BU autonomy is real)
Centralized or Hub-and-Spoke
Is your primary concern speed-to-market over governance consistency?
Federated (but risk of governance gaps)
Hub-and-Spoke or Centralized
Are you in Year 1 or 2 of enterprise GenAI adoption?
Centralized or early Hub-and-Spoke (establish platform first)
More mature models are viable

The pattern is not permanent. Organizations typically start Centralized in Year 1 (establish the platform and governance), transition to Hub-and-Spoke in Year 2 (add BU guild structure), and selectively federate mature BUs in Year 3+. The org design should evolve with the capability.
Cross-Cutting Roles: Where They Live

Three roles cause the most org design confusion because they don't fit cleanly into hub or spoke: AI Product Managers, Data Scientists, and AI Security Specialists. Getting their placement wrong creates coordination debt that compounds quickly.
AI Product Manager

The AI PM is responsible for the product strategy and roadmap of a GenAI feature — not its engineering. The AI PM works with BU product leaders to define the use case, acceptance criteria, and success metrics; works with guild engineers on feasibility; and works with the CoE on risk classification.
Placement option
Pros
Cons
When it works
Embedded in BU product team
Closest to the customer; aligned with BU roadmap; no shared-service queue
May lack AI-specific knowledge; isolated from other AI PMs
BU has > 3 active GenAI use cases; BU PM is AI-literate
Shared CoE PM pool
Deep AI expertise; cross-BU pattern visibility; feeds directly into platform roadmap
Queue for BU access; less BU-context depth; bottleneck at scale
BU has < 2 active use cases; Year 1 of AI adoption
Center of Excellence + BU rotation
Best of both: AI depth + BU context; rotation builds enterprise-wide network
Complex logistics; risk of role ambiguity during transitions
Mature CoE with > 3 BUs; annual rotation cycle established

The CoE PM pool is a trap at scale: if every BU's GenAI roadmap requires a shared PM, the CoE becomes a product management bottleneck as well as a platform one. The target state is BU-embedded AI PMs who are certified by the CoE and participate in an informal AI PM community — not a centralized PM function.
Data Scientists

Data scientists in GenAI programs do three things: evaluate model quality, fine-tune or adapt models, and build evaluation datasets. These roles are different from classical ML model training and require different skills.
Evaluation-focused DS
— should be embedded in the BU closest to the ground-truth data and use-case quality criteria; they build the eval harness for their BU's use cases using the CoE's eval infrastructure
Fine-tuning / adaptation DS
— should be in the CoE or closely coordinated with it; fine-tuned models are shared assets that go into the model registry and must pass the same review as external model additions
Synthetic data / dataset DS
— typically BU-embedded; they understand the BU's data distribution and edge cases that no generic dataset captures
AI Security Specialist

AI security (prompt injection, jailbreak testing, model exfiltration, adversarial inputs) is a specialized skill that most orgs cannot staff in every BU. The right placement is in the Security team, with a dotted line to the CoE for AI-specific policy:
The Security team owns the AI threat model and adversarial testing methodology
The CoE requests AI security reviews as part of the Tier 2 gate process (the Security team delivers them on SLA)
Guild leads in each BU are trained to recognize prompt injection and jailbreak patterns; they are the first line of defense, not the specialists

A common mistake is placing the AI Security Specialist in the CoE. This makes the CoE responsible for offensive security work (red-teaming) which is a different discipline from platform engineering and governance — it creates role confusion and dilutes the CoE's focus.
Talent Acquisition and Skill Matrix
The hub-and-spoke model requires two distinct talent tracks that should not be conflated in job descriptions or compensation structures:
Track
Where
Must-have skills
Nice-to-have
Anti-patterns
Hub (CoE) engineer
Central team
LLMOps, API gateway design, observability, platform SRE, security guardrails
ML background, data engineering, governance frameworks
Hiring generalist "ML engineers" without platform ops experience
Spoke (Guild) engineer
BU-embedded
Application engineering, prompt engineering, BU domain knowledge, RAG implementation
Fine-tuning, data pipeline engineering, product sense
Hiring PhDs for application feature work; expecting guild engineers to also run the platform
Skill Gap Assessment Template

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class SkillAssessment:
    """
    Run this assessment per engineer when establishing a BU guild.
    Scores: 0 = no exposure, 1 = basic, 2 = practitioner, 3 = expert
    """
    engineer_name: str
    role_target: str  # "guild_lead" | "guild_member"

    # Core GenAI skills
    prompt_engineering: int = 0        # System prompts, few-shot, chain-of-thought
    rag_implementation: int = 0        # Chunking, embedding, retrieval, reranking
    llm_api_integration: int = 0       # OpenAI/Anthropic SDK, error handling, retries
    evaluation_design: int = 0         # Designing evals, writing test cases, metrics

    # Platform skills (guild lead focus)
    llmops_familiarity: int = 0        # Experiment tracking, deployment pipelines
    observability: int = 0             # Tracing, logging, latency monitoring
    cost_optimization: int = 0         # Token budgeting, model routing, batching

    # Governance skills (guild lead required)
    risk_classification: int = 0       # Understanding Tier 2 triggers
    pii_data_handling: int = 0         # Data classification, prompt sanitization
    ai_ethics_basics: int = 0          # Fairness, bias, transparency

    def guild_lead_readiness(self) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
        """Returns (ready, gaps). Guild leads need >= 2 in governance skills."""
        gaps = []
        if self.risk_classification < 2:
            gaps.append("risk_classification (complete Responsible AI certification)")
        if self.pii_data_handling < 2:
            gaps.append("pii_data_handling (complete Data Privacy for GenAI module)")
        if self.prompt_engineering < 2:
            gaps.append("prompt_engineering (complete CoE Prompt Engineering course)")
        if self.evaluation_design < 1:
            gaps.append("evaluation_design (shadow CoE model evaluation review)")
        return len(gaps) == 0, gaps

Run this assessment for every guild candidate before confirming their role. Guild leads with gaps in governance skills — specifically risk classification and PII handling — should complete the required CoE certifications before taking on their first Tier 2 intake screening. The assessment is not a bar to hiring; it is an onboarding planning tool.
Org Design and Regulatory Context
Regulatory requirements directly constrain org design options in some industries. These are not suggestions — they are hard constraints that override the general guidance:
Sector
Key regulation
Org design constraint
Implication
Financial services
SR 11-7 (US Fed), EBA guidelines (EU), MAS FEAT (SG)
Model risk management function must be independent of model development
AI ethics / model validation cannot report to the CoE or to any team that builds or operates the models being validated
Healthcare
HIPAA, FDA SaMD guidance, EU MDR for AI-assisted diagnostics
Software as a Medical Device (SaMD) AI requires a clinical validation function
Guild teams building SaMD features need a clinical quality assurance liaison; this person must be in the regulatory affairs org, not the CoE
EU-regulated
EU AI Act (in force August 2026)
High-risk AI systems require conformity assessment; designated responsible person must be identified
The CoE's AI Ethics Lead typically becomes the designated responsible person for Annex III use cases; this is a named individual accountability, not a team accountability
Government / public sector
OMB AI policies (US), NIST AI RMF
AI impact assessments required for significant decisions
Tier 2 gate must include a formal AI impact assessment step for use cases affecting the public; the CoE process must produce an auditable artifact

If your organization operates in any of these sectors, the org design discussion must include Legal, Risk, and Compliance from the start — not as reviewers after the design is done. In regulated sectors, the org design is a compliance artifact as much as an operational one.
Common Org Design Failures
Failure
How it presents
Fix
Org design without governance authority
Hub team has no charter; BUs ignore it; inconsistent compliance
Ratify charter before standing up the hub; CoE must have Tier 1 binding authority to be effective
Hub team tries to own BU applications
Hub becomes a feature factory; backlog grows; BUs wait months for features
Hard boundary: hub owns platform and standards; BU owns application logic
Guild leads have no protected time
Guild members are 100% tasked on BU features; CoE participation drops to zero
Charter must specify minimum 20% time allocation; enforce via guild lead performance criteria
Hub grows into a large team
Hub headcount reaches 30+; begins to look like a second platform team; becomes political
Hub should stay lean (< 15 people for most orgs); scale by empowering spokes, not growing hub
Federated with weak policy office
Each BU builds incompatible tooling; no shared evaluation standards; governance gaps discovered in audit
Federated only works if the policy office has real enforcement authority and regular compliance checks
Summary

The target-state org design for enterprise GenAI delivery is hub-and-spoke for most organizations: a small, senior CoE hub owns the shared platform, standards, and governance; embedded AI guilds in each BU translate those standards into BU-specific implementations. The transition takes 12–18 months and requires explicit governance authority in the CoE charter before the hub can function.
Choose hub-and-spoke as the default target state; use centralized only in Year 1 or in highly regulated orgs with < 4 BUs
Hub size: 8–15 people for most orgs (CoE lead, architect, ethics lead, 2–5 MLOps engineers, PM, advisory architects)
Spoke size: 1 guild lead per BU; 2–8 engineers depending on BU maturity
Guild lead reporting structure: solid line to BU engineering lead; dotted line to CoE lead for governance accountability
Transition in three phases: establish hub (months 1–6), establish spokes (months 4–12), optimize and automate (month 12+)
